name: Post-Prompt Workflows Improve Mathematics Simulated-Learner Authenticity
statement: Cao, Nguyen, and colleagues show that authenticity of synthetic mathematics learner responses improves when generation moves beyond few-shot prompting, but different workflows create different pedagogical affordances and limitations rather than one generally superior model choice.
population_or_context: Fifth-grade fraction simulated learner for preservice mathematics teacher practice.
quantitative_note: The paper reports significant McNemar-test improvements for cognition and language authenticity for fine-tuning, multi-agent, and DPO workflows against few-shot prompting; DPO reached 100% language and 88.7% cognition authenticity descriptively in the combined set, with no significant GLMM differences among the three post-prompt approaches.

source_locator.section: Abstract; Method; Results 4.1; Discussion and Conclusion
source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase_note: Extraction uses the paper's workflow descriptions, sample construction, coding procedure, and reported authenticity results.

caveats: Authenticity is evaluated for teacher learning, not scorer validation.
The comparison is workflow-specific and does not isolate every model-family effect.
The single-task, single-agent context limits transfer to constructed-response assessment.
evaluation_method: Researcher-coded cognition and language authenticity, McNemar tests, GLMMs, and qualitative teacher/researcher interviews.
limitations: No automated scoring model is trained or validated.
Human authenticity ratings are purpose-specific to teacher noticing.
